narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, we know how much heart and determination you've poured into this journey, and it's tough to hear that your application has been rejected based on our predictive model's assessment. The model looks at patterns from thousands of past students to forecast the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, and for you, it estimated a 41% chance of failure—high enough to tip the decision against admission, as the school prioritizes applicants predicted to succeed right away. Your profile includes a Year 1 school decile of 2, a Year 3 decile of 1, low family income, an LSAT score of 27, an undergraduate GPA of 2.60, and plans to attend part-time, which stand out when compared to typical students who often have higher deciles around 5-6, LSATs near 37, GPAs over 3.2, middle-to-higher family incomes, and full-time status. The most influential factor pulling in a positive direction—toward predicting you'd pass—was your low family income, which the model associates with resilience and success in similar past cases. However, your Year 3 decile had the strongest push toward predicting failure, as it's well below the average and signals challenges in later academic performance relative to peers. Your LSAT score and undergraduate GPA also helped by nudging the prediction toward passing, though not enough to outweigh the negatives, while your Year 1 decile and part-time plans added slight concerns since full-time students dominate the successful group. Overall, these elements combined to make your predicted risk higher than what the school deems acceptable for admission. It's not a reflection of your potential as a Black woman breaking barriers in law—many with similar backgrounds have triumphed by addressing key gaps. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your academic deciles through additional coursework or grad school, and exploring full-time options if feasible, as these could dramatically shift future predictions. We encourage you to seek mentorship from successful Black attorneys or programs tailored for underrepresented students to build your edge. You're capable of rewriting this story—keep pushing, and reapply stronger. With support, your next application could tell a very different tale.
